operating_state: Chevron completed the Hess Corporation acquisition on July 18, 2025, adding 450-500 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day to production in Q3. The acquisition generated an estimated after-tax loss of $200-400 million in Q3 2025, primarily driven by integration costs and severance expenses. Excluding transaction-related costs, Hess operations contributed $50-150 million to adjusted earnings during the partial quarter.
cost_structure: The Hess acquisition increased depreciation, depletion and amortization by $1.2-1.4 billion in Q3 due to purchase price allocation impacts. Severance and transaction costs totaled $350-450 million after-tax, with $166 million in Q3 accruals split between selling, general and administrative expenses ($114 million) and upstream operating expenses ($52 million). The company expects approximately half of severance cash outflows to occur in Q3, with the remainder within twelve months.
material_events: Chevron completed the $53 billion Hess Corporation acquisition on July 18, 2025, with Hess becoming a wholly-owned subsidiary. John B. Hess was appointed to Chevron's board of directors on July 28, 2025. Chevron USA issued $5.5 billion in debt securities across seven tranches on August 13, 2025, including both fixed and floating rate notes maturing between 2027 and 2035. The company completed asset sales in Malaysia/Thailand generating $410 million in proceeds.
macro_exposures: No material new macro exposures were disclosed this period beyond standard energy sector risks.
forward_outlook: Management expects to realize synergies from the Hess transaction in future quarters, with integration activities substantially complete by end of 2026. The company anticipates working capital outflows of $0.5-1.5 billion in Q3 2025 related to the acquisition. Share count is expected to reach approximately 2 billion shares outstanding at September 30, 2025.
uncertainty_profile: Asset retirement obligations increased to $13.9 billion, including $1.7 billion assumed from the Hess acquisition. The company entered into non-binding agreements with John B. Hess regarding potential transfers of Hess toy truck business, aircraft assets, and trademark rights, with valuations subject to independent appraisals. Integration risks remain around achieving projected Hess acquisition synergies and managing the combined operations effectively.
